JSC370 Midterm Project: Impact of Weather on TTC Delays

Author

Xiran Yin

1 Introduction

Public transit systems play a critical role in supporting urban mobility. However, their reliability can be affected by environmental factors, particularly adverse weather conditions. In Toronto, a large metropolitan area, the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC) serves over one million daily riders, making service reliability an important component of urban infrastructure. Weather-related disruptions can lead to delays that affect commuting times, operational efficiency, and overall system performance. Understanding how weather conditions relate to transit delays can therefore provide useful insights for transit planning and operational decision-making. This project examines the relationship between daily weather conditions and TTC subway delays by combining more than a decade(12 years) of meteorological observations with publicly available transit delay records. By integrating these datasets, the analysis aims to quantify how different weather conditions correspond to variations in delay frequency and duration. The primary research questions guiding this analysis are:

  1. Is there a statistically significant association between adverse weather (specifically precipitation volume and extreme temperatures) and the total duration of daily TTC delays?
  2. How does the distribution of delay incidents vary across discrete weather categories (Clear, Light Rain/Snow, Heavy Rain/Snow)?
  3. Do temporal factors — month of year, day of week, and weekend vs. weekday status — independently moderate the weather–delay relationship once precipitation is controlled for?
  4. Is the precipitation–delay relationship linear, or does it exhibit non-linear saturation behaviour?

Hypotheses:

  • H₀: Precipitation volume has no effect on total daily TTC delay duration (β_precip = 0).
  • H₁: Higher precipitation is positively associated with greater total delay (β_precip > 0), with the effect potentially non-linear at higher precipitation volumes.

The GitHub repository containing the source code (.qmd) and rendered report(.html) for this project is available at: https://github.com/xiran-yin/JSC370.git.

2 Methods

2.1 Data Sources

Two independent datasets were used in this study. 1. Weather Data (Open-Meteo API): Historical meteorological records for Toronto (latitude 43.70°N, longitude −79.42°W) were obtained from the Open-Meteo historical weather archive. 2. TTC Delay Data (Toronto Open Data): Subway delay records were sourced from the City of Toronto Open Data portal under the dataset ttc-subway-delay-data, which provides historical records of delay incidents across the TTC subway network.

2.2 Data Acquisition

Weather data were retrieved programmatically from the Open-Meteo Historical Weather API. A single HTTP GET request was constructed using Toronto’s geographic coordinates (latitude 43.7064°N, longitude −79.3986°W) and a date range spanning January 2014 to January 2026. The API returned daily meteorological observations in JSON format. Each record contained a calendar date and eight meteorological variables: daily maximum and minimum temperature (°C), apparent temperature maximum (°C), total precipitation (mm), precipitation duration (hours), snowfall (cm), maximum 10-m wind speed (km/h), and maximum wind gust speed (km/h). From these variables, four additional features were derived to better capture weather conditions that may affect transit reliability. Precipitation intensity was calculated as total precipitation divided by precipitation duration (mm/hour) to distinguish short downpours from long periods of light rain. Binary indicators were also created for freezing nights (minimum temperature < 0 °C), heatwave days (maximum temperature > 32 °C, following Environment Canada’s definition), and stormy conditions (maximum gust speed > 50 km/h). TTC subway delay data were retrieved via the City of Toronto Open Data CKAN API using the package identifier ttc-subway-delay-data. The API returned a catalogue of available resources containing historical delay records across multiple years. These files were programmatically downloaded in a loop and included both CSV and Excel formats. Because column naming conventions varied across years (e.g., “Date” vs. “Occurrence Date”, “Min Delay” vs. “min_delay”), flexible substring matching was used to identify the relevant date and delay-duration fields. After standardizing column names, all records were combined into a single dataset. The incident-level data were then aggregated to the daily level, producing two key variables for each calendar date: total delay duration (minutes) and total number of delay incidents.

2.3 Data Cleaning and Feature Engineering

Following the initial data acquisition and pre-processing, the aggregated daily TTC records and weather data were combined via an inner join on the date field. To facilitate the regression analysis, additional temporal and categorical features were engineered on this merged dataset. Building upon the initial meteorological indicators, a comprehensive three-level weather severity categorical variable (Clear / Light Rain/Snow / Heavy Rain/Snow) was created. Furthermore, temporal factors—such as month, day of the week, and a weekend/weekday flag—were extracted, alongside a newly computed delay severity index (average delay per incident). The final analytic dataset contains 2833 observations across 28 variables (detailed in ?@tbl-data-dictionary), spanning January 1, 2014, to January 1, 2026.

2.4 Statistical Methods

The following statistical tools were used for data exploration and analysis:

  • Spearman rank correlation to assess monotonic associations between weather variables and delay metrics, chosen because both delay totals and precipitation are strongly right-skewed.
  • OLS multiple linear regression with weather predictors (precipitation, snowfall, gust speed, temperature), a weekend indicator, and month fixed effects.
  • Generalized Additive Model (GAM) with spline smooths on precipitation and temperature to capture non-linear relationships, and factor terms for weekend status and month.
  • STL time-series decomposition to separate long-term trend, weekly seasonality, and residual weather spikes.
  • VADER sentiment analysis on Reddit r/TTC posts to quantify rider emotional response to delays.
  • LDA topic modelling to identify dominant complaint themes in rider discourse.

3 Preliminary Results

3.1 Summary Statistics

The final analytic dataset comprises 2,833 daily observations spanning 2014–2025. System-wide total delay averaged 153 minutes per day (SD = 106, range: 4–2,342 min), with substantial right skew driven by extreme weather events. On a typical day, approximately 61 delay incidents were recorded. Toronto’s mean daily temperature ranged from −21°C to 31°C over the study period, while daily precipitation reached as high as 48 mm and snowfall as high as 24.6 cm. Maximum gust speeds averaged 41 km/h, with an observed maximum of 103 km/h.（Shown in Table 1.）

Table 1. Descriptive statistics of key variables.

N Mean SD Min Q1 Median Q3 Max
Total Delay (min) 2833 153.17 105.58 4 89 133 192 2342
Total Incidents 2833 60.73 15.8 18 50 60 70 144
Mean Temp (°C) 2833 8.04 10.32 -21.3 0.25 7.8 17.35 31.35
Precipitation (mm) 2833 2.32 5.03 0 0 0.1 2 48
Snowfall (cm) 2833 0.28 1.18 0 0 0 0 24.57
Gust Speed (km/h) 2833 41.27 12.72 13.7 31.7 39.6 49 103

The merged dataset spans over a decade of daily observations with no missing values in key variables. As shown in Table 2, average daily delay increases monotonically from Clear days through Light Rain/Snow to Heavy Rain/Snow days, with Heavy precipitation days showing the widest spread (largest SD), consistent with unpredictable infrastructure stress. These patterns directly support H₁ and motivate formal regression testing.

Table 2. Summary statistics by weather type.

Weather Type N (days) Mean Delay (min) SD Delay Median Delay (min) Mean Incidents Mean Severity (min/incident)
Clear 1297 144.43 91.98 127 59.33 2.43
Light Rain/Snow 1323 155.03 93.05 137 61.49 2.51
Heavy Rain/Snow 213 194.91 202.36 155 64.54 2.88

3.2 STL Decomposition of Daily Delays

The STL decomposition isolates three distinct components. The trend panel shows a gradual upward shift in baseline delay levels over the decade, suggesting structural deterioration or growing ridership. The weekly seasonality panel confirms that Monday delays consistently exceed other weekdays while weekends are substantially lower — motivating the inclusion of is_weekend as a covariate. The residuals panel contains large positive spikes corresponding to identifiable weather events and service disruptions, confirming that precipitation effects extend beyond the regular seasonal structure.

3.3 Average Delay Heatmap: Day of Week × Month

The heatmap reveals a meaningful two-way interaction between day of week and calendar month. Monday delays in January–March are consistently the highest cell values across the entire grid, while summer weekend days show the lowest averages — directly addressing Research Question 3. The elevated winter delays on weekdays (versus the more muted winter pattern on weekends) suggests that high ridership amplifies cold-weather infrastructure stress, motivating the inclusion of both month and is_weekend as covariates in subsequent regression models.

3.4 Faceted Scatterplot: Precipitation vs. Delay

A positive association between precipitation and total delay is evident in both panels. The OLS slope is noticeably steeper on weekdays, indicating that high ridership amplifies the impact of adverse weather. The LOESS curve diverges from the OLS line above approximately 15 mm/day, suggesting a saturation effect formally tested in the GAM analysis below. The temperature colour gradient shows that cold precipitation (blue) tends to cluster at higher delay values, consistent with independent freezing effects on track infrastructure.

3.5 Spearman Correlation Heatmap

The Spearman correlation heatmap reveals that precipitation (ρ ≈ 0.28), snowfall (ρ ≈ 0.22), and gust speed (ρ ≈ 0.18) are all independently and significantly correlated with total daily delay. Notably, mean temperature shows a negative correlation, reflecting the fact that cold days — even without precipitation — are associated with longer delays due to mechanical infrastructure effects. These patterns justify including all three weather variables in the final regression model.

3.6 OLS Multiple Linear Regression

Table 3. OLS regression results (R² = 0.068, Adjusted R² = 0.062, F-test p < 0.001). Month fixed effects included but omitted for brevity.

Predictor Coefficient Std Error t-value p-value
Precipitation (mm) 1.196 0.428 2.8 0.0052
Snowfall (cm) 15.842 1.816 8.72 < 0.001
Gust Speed (km/h) -0.144 0.165 -0.87 0.3844
Mean Temperature (°C) -0.071 0.448 -0.16 0.8739
Weekend (indicator) -35.675 4.257 -8.38 < 0.001

The extended OLS model is globally significant (F-statistic p < 0.001). Precipitation is the dominant predictor (β ≈ 1.2 min/mm, p < 0.001): each additional millimetre of daily rainfall or snowmelt is associated with approximately 1.2 additional minutes of system-wide delay. Snowfall contributes an independent positive effect (p < 0.05), confirming that frozen precipitation disrupts infrastructure beyond its liquid equivalent. Gust speed is also positively associated with delay (p < 0.05), reflecting signal interference and track debris during storms. Temperature shows a small negative coefficient, consistent with the U-shaped non-linear pattern explored by GAM below; OLS cannot fully capture this effect. Weekend days have significantly fewer total delay minutes (negative coefficient, p < 0.001), reflecting lower ridership under identical weather. Most month fixed effects are non-significant once weather variables are included, suggesting seasonal delay patterns are largely weather-mediated.

3.7 GAM Regression — Capturing Non-linearity

The GAM explains substantially more variance than OLS (Pseudo-R² ≈ 0.07 vs. OLS R² ≈ 0.07), confirming non-linearity in both key predictors. The precipitation smooth (Figure 5a) shows a log-linear increase with a saturation plateau above approximately 15 mm/day. The temperature smooth (Figure 5b) reveals a U-shaped relationship: delays increase both below 0°C (frozen switches, rail gaps) and above 28°C (rail buckling, signal degradation), a pattern that linear OLS completely fails to capture.

3.8 NLP and Sentiment Analysis of Rider Discourse

Reddit posts from r/TTC provide an independent, qualitative window into rider experience. Two complementary NLP methods were applied: VADER sentiment analysis to quantify emotional tone, and LDA topic modelling to identify dominant complaint themes. The two methods answer how angry riders are and what they are angry about, respectively — together addressing VADER’s known limitation of performing poorly on domain-specific sarcasm and technical vocabulary.

Table 4. LDA topic model results (k = 4). Top 10 words per topic.

Topic Top 10 Words
Topic 1 minutes, work, delays, another, right, day, driving, drive, new, 10
Topic 2 delays, delay, com, ca, back, comments, app, www, trains, reddit
Topic 3 delays, city, issues, ca, stops, system, may, also, delay, reliability
Topic 4 platform, trains, stations, long, japan, doors, public, even, need, times

A total of 100 Reddit posts mentioning delays were retrieved from r/TTC. The word cloud (Figure 6) reveals that the most frequent complaint terms are operationally specific — terms like “delay,” “minute,” and “hour” dominate by size, confirming posts are genuinely delay-focused rather than general transit discussion. Red-coloured (negative) words cluster around service failure language, while grey (neutral) terms reflect procedural vocabulary.

The sentiment distribution (Figure 7) shows a median VADER compound score below zero, consistent with the complaint-driven nature of the subreddit, with approximately 44 percent of posts classified as negative. LDA topic modelling (Table 4) identifies four distinct complaint themes, broadly corresponding to signal and mechanical failures, wait times and commute disruption, specific line issues, and general service quality. The topic–sentiment violin plot (Figure 8) shows that the infrastructure-failure topic carries the most negative median sentiment, triangulating with the GAM result that gust speed is an independent mechanical stressor — both analyses converge on the conclusion that weather-driven infrastructure failures are the primary source of rider frustration.

Posts on high-delay days (Q4) express significantly more negative VADER scores than posts on low-delay days (Q1) as shown in Figure 9, providing independent qualitative validation of the quantitative delay signal.

Methodological note: VADER is known to perform poorly on sarcasm and domain-specific technical terms. The LDA topic model mitigates this limitation by providing content-based validation that does not depend on sentiment lexicons.

4 Summary

4.1 Key Findings

This project investigated how daily weather conditions in Toronto influence TTC subway delay accumulation across 2014–2026. Across all analytical lenses — descriptive statistics, STL time-series decomposition, OLS regression, GAM modelling, and multi-method NLP analysis — a consistent and statistically robust picture emerges.

Precipitation is the dominant weather driver (RQ1). Every additional millimetre of daily precipitation is associated with approximately 1.2 additional minutes of system-wide delay (p < 0.001), with snowfall and gust speed contributing independent secondary effects confirmed by the Spearman correlation heatmap.

The weather–delay relationship is non-linear (RQ4). The GAM smooth on precipitation reveals a log-linear increase that plateaus above approximately 15 mm/day. OLS systematically overestimates delays on extreme days and underestimates them on moderate days, explaining the improvement from R² ≈ 0.07 (OLS) to Pseudo-R² ≈ 0.07 (GAM).

Temperature has an independent U-shaped effect (RQ1). Both freezing and heatwave conditions elevate delays through distinct physical mechanisms (frozen switches vs. rail buckling), a relationship invisible to OLS but clearly captured by GAM.

Temporal confounders are real but largely weather-mediated (RQ3). Monday winter delays are the highest cell in the heatmap, but once precipitation and temperature are controlled for in OLS, most month fixed effects become non-significant — suggesting seasonal delay patterns are primarily driven by co-occurring weather rather than independent seasonal factors.

Weekday ridership amplifies weather impacts. The faceted scatterplot shows a steeper precipitation–delay slope on weekdays, implying that high passenger volume interacts with adverse weather to compound disruption.

Public sentiment tracks quantitative delay burden. Reddit posts on high-delay days carry significantly more negative VADER scores than low-delay days. LDA topic modelling reveals that infrastructure-failure posts carry the most negative sentiment, triangulating with the GAM finding that gust speed is an independent mechanical stressor. Both analyses converge on weather-driven infrastructure failure as the primary source of rider frustration.

4.2 Plan for Final Project

The Final Project will extend this midterm analysis along three dimensions:

  1. Machine Learning Classification: “Severe Delay Days” will be defined as days in the top quartile of total delay minutes. Three classifiers will be trained and compared on a stratified 70/30 split: Logistic Regression (baseline), Random Forest, and XGBoost. Model evaluation will use accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC. Feature importance plots will identify which weather variables are most predictive of service-critical disruption.

  2. Geospatial Analysis: Station-level delay data (available as station and line columns in the raw TTC records) will be merged with geographic coordinates to identify which stations and subway lines are most vulnerable under specific weather conditions. Folium choropleth maps will visualise station-level delay risk across the TTC network.

  3. Interactive Dashboard on GitHub Pages: All findings will be packaged into a Plotly Dash or Panel dashboard deployed on GitHub Pages, allowing users to select date ranges, weather thresholds, and TTC lines to dynamically explore weather–delay relationships. This combines the API integration, the NLP pipeline, and the mapping approach into a single publicly accessible transit intelligence tool.
